retrieved_docs.title: Cone Snail Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: An additional, but concerning, potential use of conotoxins is the capability of creating bioterrorism agents. Most toxins are relatively simple combinations of peptides, so as they are studied, these combinations are relatively easy to recreate within a laboratory setting. Potential bioterrorism concerns include the utilization of conotoxins in food source contamination and aerosolization of conotoxins for wide dispersal.

retrieved_docs.title: Funnel-Web Spider Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The venom of FWSs is complex, containing thousands of peptide toxins. Among these, the δ-hexatoxins are primarily responsible for symptomatic envenomation. [8] [9] [10] Specifically, the δ-atracotoxins robustotoxin (Ar1) and versutoxin (Hv1a) are amino acid peptides with 42 to 44 residues and 4 disulfide linkages. [11] [12] These neurotoxins have highly preserved homologous sequences and activity in mammals and insects. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ciguatoxin is a potent sodium channel poison in mammals. The toxin causes sustained sodium channel activation and blocks potassium channels. These actions combined lead to membrane depolarization and spontaneous, repetitive action potential firing (see Image . Sites and Modes of Action of Ciguatoxins).

retrieved_docs.title: Amatoxin Mushroom Toxicity -- Introduction -- Lepiota group:
retrieved_docs.content: Lepiota helveola Lepiota chlorophyllum Lepiota brunneolilacea Lepiota josserandi Lepiota fulvella Lepiota subincarnata Lepiota brunneoincarnata

retrieved_docs.title: Shellfish and Dinoflagellate Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Brevetoxins bind with high affinity to the same voltage-gated sodium channels but at receptor site 5, located in the intramembranous portion of the ion channel. In contrast to saxitoxin, brevetoxins induce sodium ion influx. The increased sodium conductance results in membrane depolarization, spontaneous firing, and neuroexcitation. [4] Brevetoxin's chemical structure is similar to ciguatoxin, and both molecules have similar receptor activity. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Increased sodium influx damages excitable cells, particularly the neurons and cardiac myocytes. Swelling of the axons, nerve terminals, and perisynaptic Schwann cells occurs in the neurons. Meanwhile, in the cardiac myocytes, animal studies demonstrate that sodium channel-mediated ciguatoxin action influences cardiac contractility. Moderate ciguatoxin concentrations have indirect and direct positive inotropic actions, while high toxin levels produce negative inotropic effects. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Palytoxin Keratitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Palytoxin (PTX) is a large naturally occurring toxin initially isolated from zoanthids (i.e., soft corals) in the genus Palythoa and has since been found in various marine life including microalgae. [1] PTX is a highly deadly polyhydroxylated and partially desaturated non-proteinaceous carbon backbone with a molecular weight of 2680 kilodaltons and a lethal dose (LD) of 150 ng/kg body weight in mice when given intravenously. [2] It is among the largest nonpolymeric natural products. It was first synthesized in totality in 1994, decades after its initial discovery. The toxin is released from organelles called nematocysts, which release spiral thread-like structures with ‘barbs’ that can pierce tissue upon contact. [3] These soft corals are often present in aquariums due to their beauty and relatively low maintenance. Though not all species are known to produce a toxin, the exact identification of the specific genus and species is often not possible; thus, all should be treated as toxic. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Amatoxin Mushroom Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of amatoxin mushroom poisoning. Describe the clinical presentation of a patient having amatoxin mushroom poisoning. Illustrate the treatment and management options available for amatoxin mushroom poisoning. Discuss interprofessional team strategies for improving care and outcomes in patients with amatoxin mushroom poisoning. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Palytoxin Keratitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initial therapy should include the removal of the toxin from the eye by rinsing or instillation of artificial tears. [7] Ruiz et al. add that each eye should undergo irrigation individually. Their report describes a case in which toxin was squirted only into the right eye, following which the patient immediately irrigated their eye with tap water for several minutes and then developed bilateral symptoms. They concluded that irrigating the right eye potentially brought the skin and left eye into contact with the toxin. [10] Also, there have been suggestions that the concentration of, and exposure time to PTX increases with contact lens usage [4] ; thus, immediate clean removal of contact lenses is likely beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: Cone Snail Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Conus venom is a complex mixture of compounds that cause paralysis through multiple neuromuscular blocking steps. The combinations of peptides that comprise venom vary between species, and it has been estimated that each cone snail harbors over 100,000 different bioactive compounds within its venom. The complexity of this poison and the variation in target pathways has prevented the production of effective anti-venom. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Cone Snail Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the rarity of cone snail envenomation and the lack of data, it is uncertain whether death is the result of respiratory toxicity, cardiovascular toxicity, or a combination of the 2. The effects of envenomation vary based on the specific peptides within the venom and as a result, the effects are largely unpredictable. These toxins have a variety of neuromuscular effects through glutamate, adrenergic (chi conotoxin), serotonin, and cholinergic pathways. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Lionfish, Scorpionfish, and Stonefish Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the frequency of encounters with poisonous fish. Describe the venom producing organs associated with lionfish, scorpionfish, and stonefish. Outline the management of a patient that has received envenomation from lionfish, scorpionfish, and stonefish. Explain the role of interprofessional team members in collaborating to provide well-coordinated care and enhance outcomes for affected patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Funnel-Web Spider Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: FWSs consist of 36 Australian species classified based on anatomical differences and 38 related non-Australian species in the genus Macrothele . Most FWSs are ground-dwelling, including the well-known species Atrax robustus (Sydney FWS) and Hadronyche infensa (Toowoomba FWS). Tree-dwelling species include the Hadryonyche cerberea (Southern tree-swelling FWS) and Hadronyche formidabilis (Northern tree-dwelling FWS). [4] [5] Data suggest that tree-dwelling species are associated with much higher rates of severe envenomations than ground-dwelling FWSs. Of these spiders, only 6 species have been identified as containing δ-hexatoxins. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The toxin causes pruritus owing to deranged low-frequency discharge in C-polymodal nociceptive fibers (cutaneous afferent unmyelinated fibers), which are not normally active in undamaged skin at normal temperatures. [17] Cold allodynia or cold temperature inversion is due to increased nerve depolarization of type Aδ and C fibers.

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ciguatoxin is initially introduced into the flesh of herbivorous fish through the consumption of ciguatoxin-producing dinoflagellates. The toxin accumulates through predation until it reaches the top-tier predatory fish in sufficient concentrations. Consumption of contaminated fish harms humans. The fish that most commonly cause ciguatera toxicity are barracuda, grouper, moray eel, amberjack, sea bass, sturgeon, parrotfish, surgeonfish, and red snapper. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic peptides (cyklické peptidy) constitute a class of bioactive compounds that include eptifibatid as a child molecule, and they are also linked through mapped relationships to both isaridin E and the paclitaxel‑EC‑1 conjugate, highlighting the structural and functional interconnections among these agents in pharmacological research and development.

Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

Collectively, these interrelated classifications, structural analogies, biosynthetic relationships and therapeutic categories provide a comprehensive framework for drug development, pharmacologic targeting, resistance monitoring and clinical decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 914
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is a depsipeptide that functions as a DNA minor‑groove binding agent; this mechanistic class is classified within the trabektedin family. The trabektedin family also includes other plant alkaloids and natural products that are listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification. Recognizing that Actinomycin and these additional natural products share the trabektedin designation highlights their common ability to bind DNA in the minor groove, a property that underlies their therapeutic actions and potential toxicities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 103
retrieved_docs.content: interlinked qualifiers and hierarchical links provide a unified framework that maps diverse biochemical entities—hormones, peptides, enzymes, microbial products, toxins, and synthetic drugs—to their biosynthetic origins and physiological functions, supporting integrated understanding of metabolic regulation, drug action, and disease pathology. Clinically, deslanosid (a cardiac glycoside) may be administered for decreased cardiac output, exemplifying the therapeutic relevance of these qualifier relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1371
retrieved_docs.content: Ziconotide is classified as a neuroprotective substance, indicating that it belongs to the group of agents that protect neuronal tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1321
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is classified as a depsipeptide, indicating that it belongs to a broader chemical family of compounds that contain both peptide and ester linkages. This broader relationship situates actinomycin within a group of biologically active molecules whose structural features influence their pharmacological properties and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 99
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is classified as a cyclic peptide that also functions as a DNA minor‑groove binding agent, linking it to the broader family of depsipeptides. Within the hierarchy of peptide‑based natural products, cyclic peptides serve as a parent class for compounds such as eptifibatid, while being mapped to specific molecules including isaridin E, the paclitaxel‑EC‑1 conjugate, and a glutamyl‑histidyl‑phenylalanyl‑arginyl‑tryptophyl‑glycyl‑lysyl‑prolyl‑valyl‑glycinamide cyclic peptide. These cyclic peptides trace their lineage to several parent groups: kapreomycinsulfát, streptograminy, and viomycin. Streptograminy, in turn, belong to the larger pharmacological category of macrolides, lincosamides and streptogramins. Actinomycin’s role as a DNA minor‑groove binding agent places it within the trabectedin family, a group that also encompasses other plant alkaloids and natural products catalogued in the ATC system. Together, these relationships illustrate how Actinomycin and related cyclic peptides are interconnected through shared structural features, biosynthetic origins, and mechanisms of DNA interaction, informing both drug classification and therapeutic strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 5 -- Community 2944
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier denotes that chlortetracyklin is associated with toxicita, and similarly cholerové vakcíny are linked to toxicita; recognizing the toxic potential of both chlortetracyklin and cholera vaccines is essential for safe use and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Capsicum is closely linked to capsaicin, the pungent compound that gives the plant its characteristic heat. Capsaicin serves as the parent molecule for the Solanaceae alkaloids, indicating that it is a fundamental chemical precursor within this family of plant-derived alkaloids. In pharmacological classification, capsaicin is regarded both as a biological toxin and as an antipruritic agent, reflecting its dual role as a potentially harmful substance and as a therapeutic compound used to alleviate itching. Together, these connections illustrate how the active ingredient of Capsicum fits into broader chemical families and therapeutic categories, informing both toxicological assessment and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1265
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is identified as a cytotoxic antibiotic and related substance, and it also has a broader relationship with depsipeptidy, indicating that Actinomycin belongs to or is associated with the larger chemical class of depsipeptide compounds. This connection underscores Actinomycin’s role as a potent anticancer agent within a broader family of peptide‑derived antibiotics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2738
retrieved_docs.content: Botulinum toxins (botulotoxiny), potassium chloride (chlorid draselný), chromonar, ergotamine (ergotamin), estetrol, and dipotassium clorazepate (klorazepát dvojdraselný) are all classified as poisons (otrava). These substances share the common property of being toxic agents that can cause poisoning when exposure occurs. In addition, the category of poisoning is linked to spiro compounds (spirosloučeniny), indicating that many of these poisonous agents belong to or are associated with the chemical class of spiro compounds. Recognizing that these diverse agents are toxic and may be chemically related to spiro structures is important for risk assessment, clinical management of poisoning, and the development of safety regulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2236
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria is described as having botulotoxiny as its parent, indicating a hierarchical relationship in which botulotoxiny is considered the broader or preceding entity to Bacteria.
